did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a proprietary fully convolutional neural network (a deep learning algorithm) to classify whole slide images (WSI) of dermatopathology specimens. The AI was trained to diagnose three common dermatopathology diagnoses: nodular basal cell carcinoma (BCC), dermal nevi, and seborrheic keratoses, achieving high accuracy rates (99.45%, 99.4%, and 100% respectively).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The article is labeled as an 'Original Article' and presents original experimental results from a study developing and validating a proprietary fully convolutional neural network. It details specific methods including IRB approval, data collection of whole slide images, algorithm training on three sequential studies, and reports quantitative performance metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) for diagnosing nodular basal cell carcinoma, dermal nevi, and seborrheic keratoses.

title: Diagnostic Performance of Deep Learning Algorithms Applied to Three Common Diagnoses in Dermatopathology
brief_summary: This study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a proprietary fully convolutional neural network applied to whole slide images of three common dermatopathology diagnoses: nodular basal cell carcinoma, dermal nevi, and seborrheic keratoses. The algorithm was trained and tested using de-identified hematoxylin and eosin-stained slides, with distractor diagnoses included to assess specificity. The deep learning model achieved high accuracy rates of 99.45% for nodular BCC, 99.4% for dermal nevi, and 100% for seborrheic keratoses. These results demonstrate that deep learning algorithms can accurately classify these dermatopathology specimens, potentially serving as an adjunct to diagnosis. The authors conclude that such technology may improve workflow efficiencies for dermatopathologists and laboratories.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary target diagnosis in the first study and one of the three main diagnoses evaluated. 123/124 test cases correctly classified (99.45% balanced accuracy, 100% sensitivity, 98.9% specificity).
Primary target diagnosis in the second study and one of the three main diagnoses evaluated. 113/114 test cases correctly classified (99.4% balanced accuracy, 98.8% sensitivity, 100% specificity).
Primary target diagnosis in the third study and one of the three main diagnoses evaluated. 123/123 test cases correctly classified (100% balanced accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity).

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic labels (presence or absence of specific dermatopathology diagnoses: nodular basal cell carcinoma, dermal nevus, or seborrheic keratosis) for whole slide images.
all_task_types.details: The study developed proprietary fully convolutional neural networks to classify test images as positive or negative relative to ground truth diagnosis. The model performed binary classification for three distinct diagnostic tasks: nodular BCC vs. distractors, dermal nevus vs. distractors, and seborrheic keratosis vs. distractors. Performance was evaluated using balanced accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. While the internal semantic segmentation model assigned class likelihoods (probabilities) to pixels to generate heat maps, the final output and evaluation were based on slide-level binary classification of the presence of the lesion.

datasets_used.name: Nodular BCC Algorithm Dataset (Study 1)
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset of de-identified hematoxylin and eosin-stained whole slide images (WSI) from the Dermatopathology Laboratory of Central States, scanned at x40 magnification. Used for training and testing a proprietary fully convolutional neural network to diagnose nodular basal cell carcinoma.

use.training: Training set consisted of 200 annotated nodular BCC slides and 100 distractor slides (20 each of verruca vulgaris, actinic keratosis, spongiotic dermatitis, basosquamous acanthoma, and dermal nevus).
use.testing: Test set consisted of 124 slides (83 nodular BCC, 8 verruca vulgaris, 9 actinic keratosis, 8 spongiotic dermatitis, 7 basosquamous acanthoma, 9 dermal nevus).

datasets_used.name: Dermal Nevus Algorithm Dataset (Study 2)
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset of de-identified hematoxylin and eosin-stained whole slide images (WSI) from the Dermatopathology Laboratory of Central States, scanned at x40 magnification. Used for training and testing a proprietary fully convolutional neural network to diagnose dermal nevi.

use.training: Training set consisted of 125 annotated dermal nevus slides and 100 distractor slides (20 each of fibroepithelial polyp, granuloma annulare, verruca vulgaris, dermatofibroma, and BCC variants).
use.testing: Test set consisted of 114 slides (79 dermal nevus, 6 fibroepithelial polyp, 7 granuloma annulare, 8 verruca vulgaris, 8 dermatofibroma, 6 BCC variants).

datasets_used.name: Seborrheic Keratosis Algorithm Dataset (Study 3)
datasets_used.description: A proprietary dataset of de-identified hematoxylin and eosin-stained whole slide images (WSI) from the Dermatopathology Laboratory of Central States, scanned at x40 magnification. Used for training and testing a proprietary fully convolutional neural network to diagnose seborrheic keratoses.

use.training: Training set consisted of 125 annotated seborrheic keratosis slides and 100 distractor slides (20 each of verruca vulgaris, Bowen's disease, lichenoid keratosis, molluscum contagiosum, and BCC variants).
use.testing: Test set consisted of 123 slides (83 seborrheic keratosis, 8 verruca vulgaris, 8 Bowen's disease, 8 lichenoid keratosis, 8 molluscum contagiosum, 8 BCC variants).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To examine the classifying model with different probability values (0.0–1.0) as thresholds and determine the optimal threshold value to provide the best sensitivity and specificity.
To correlate with detection accuracy of the binary detection models for nodular BCC, dermal nevus, and seborrheic keratosis.
To evaluate overall model performance, defined as the average of sensitivity and specificity.
To assess the algorithm's ability to correctly identify positive cases (sensitivity) and negative cases (specificity) against ground truth diagnosis.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and balanced accuracy, along with Receiver Operating Characteristics (ROC) analysis and Area Under the Curve (AUC) calculations.

Nodular BCC Algorithm Dataset (Study 1).notes: The single false positive was a dermal nevus incorrectly classified as nodular BCC. The distractor set included verruca vulgaris, actinic keratosis, spongiotic dermatitis, basosquamous acanthoma, and dermal nevus.

Dermal Nevus Algorithm Dataset (Study 2).notes: The single false negative was a dermal nevocellular nevus with a diffuse arrangement of nevocytes rather than nests. The distractor set included fibroepithelial polyp, granuloma annulare, verruca vulgaris, dermatofibroma, and BCC variants.

Seborrheic Keratosis Algorithm Dataset (.notes: The algorithm correctly classified 100% of the test slides. The distractor set included verruca vulgaris, Bowen's disease, lichenoid keratosis, molluscum contagiosum, and BCC variants.

architecture_used.pretraining: Derivative of a typical Visual Geometry Group (VGG) network architecture introduced by Simonyan and Zisserman; transfers learned representations by fine-tuning to the segmentation task.
architecture_used.input_size: Tiles extracted from Whole Slide Images (WSI) scanned at x40 magnification; WSI contain resolutions from x1 to x40 magnification.

architecture_details.type: Fully Convolutional Network (FCN) for semantic segmentation
architecture_details.mechanism: End-to-end, pixels-to-pixels training; hierarchical parsing of WSI to access regions at different magnification levels; tissue detection and isolation to remove background; decomposition into square tiles; binary mask creation for pixel labeling.

training_details.regularization: Convergent loss function to prevent overfitting
training_details.additional_techniques: Rule-based discrimination system using feature vectors (size, shape, texture) of contiguous regions/nests to eliminate false positives; post-processing classification model for final arbiter of truth.
training_details.hardware: Caffe framework on a machine with 32-Core processor, 60GB RAM, and four NVIDIA K520 GPUs
training_details.duration: Training and validation conducted over a period of 4 days for each algorithm

classification_or_output_head.type: Semantic segmentation model assigning class likelihood to each pixel
classification_or_output_head.output: Probability heat map (third dimension class likelihood) representing likelihood of pixel belonging to the given lesion; slide-level binary classification derived from composition of all individual patterns identified by post-processing system.

preprocessing_techniques: Tissue detection and isolation to remove background space from Whole Slide Images (WSI)
Decomposition of WSI into smaller square tiles (patch extraction)
Hierarchical parsing of WSI to access regions at different magnification levels (x1 to x40)
Creation of binary masks for pixel labeling (delineating normal vs. neoplastic/inflammatory tissue)
Annotation of positive training examples by dermatopathologists and computer scientists
Rule-based discrimination system using feature vectors (size, shape, texture) of contiguous regions/nests to eliminate false positives
conclusions: Deep learning algorithms using a proprietary fully convolutional neural network can accurately classify three common dermatopathology diagnoses: nodular basal cell carcinoma (99.45% balanced accuracy), dermal nevi (99.4% balanced accuracy), and seborrheic keratoses (100% balanced accuracy).
The study demonstrates proof of concept for using deep learning in dermatopathology, suggesting it may serve as a potential adjunct to diagnosis.
The implementation of such artificial intelligence systems may result in improved workflow efficiencies for dermatopathologists and laboratories.
The algorithms were able to distinguish target diagnoses from various distractor diagnoses (including other neoplastic and inflammatory conditions) with high sensitivity and specificity.
The study acknowledges that while the AI system is accurate, pathologists remain critical for rendering a final and accurate diagnosis.
important_remarks: The study is a proof-of-concept using a proprietary algorithm from a single commercial source (Proscia Inc.), limiting generalizability to other deep learning models or platforms.
Data was collected from a single academic dermatopathology laboratory (Dermatopathology Laboratory of Central States), introducing potential population and scanner-specific bias.
The dataset size is small (total 300 slides across three studies), which may limit the robustness of the deep learning models compared to larger multi-institutional datasets.
No external validation was performed; the model was only tested on data from the same institution where it was trained.
The study did not report formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals, relying instead on descriptive accuracy metrics.
The distractor classes were limited to five specific diagnoses per study, which may not fully represent the complexity of real-world differential diagnoses.
The algorithm achieved 100% accuracy for seborrheic keratosis, which may indicate overfitting or a lack of challenging distractors in that specific test set.
The study acknowledges that pathologists remain critical for final diagnosis, positioning the AI as an adjunct rather than a replacement.
The training and testing sets were partitioned sequentially, and the study design allowed for correction of elements between studies, which may introduce selection bias.
